comments.body: I don't want check my collection for emptiness in service layer... Why not?
The correct place for such business logic is exactly the service layer. What exactly worries you in such approach?
it's make code cleaner, not to check it every time for emptiness. It's works even if I pass empty collection, but then redundant query executed
yes, I know that. But maybe there are some techniques for such situations? Is my example "code with smell" or is it okay?

answers.body: You cannot hide individual Spring Data repository methods, all interface methods are public by design. Spring Data creates a proxy that implements your repository interface. The methods it implements must be part of that public contract. A method in an interface is effectively public for consumers (and Spring Data won’t create a query for a private interface method anyway). So annotating a private method with @Query won’t be picked up.

Usually in Spring Data repository all methods are exposed as public. Methods cant be hidden. But if you wish to hide the internal logic, you are try below work arounds:

1. You can move sensitive logic to service layer and keep the dao layer clean


@Service
public class SampleService{

@Autowired
private SampleRepo sampleRepo;

public void sampleProcess(){
    // keep you internal logic here before calling DAO layer
}
}

2. A custom interface can be created and that will be implemented by a class which will hold the sensitive logic. Now custom interface can be extended by your actual repository interface where all your public methods can be implemented


public interface SamplerepoCustom{
    void sampleInternalProcess();
}
    
public interface SamplerepoImpl implements SamplerepoCustom{
    public void sampleInternalProcess(){
        // keep you internal logic here
    };
}

public interface SampleRepo extends JpaRepository<Item,Long>, SamplerepoCustom{
    //keep your public methods here
}

title: What is correct way to hide part of spring data repository methods
body:
for example I have UserRepository and I want to make some methods of it private. What is correct way to do this?

@Repository
public interface TestRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long> {
    
    @Query("From User u join userGroups g Where g in (:userGroups)")
    Set<User> privateFindUsersBelongsToUserGroup(Collection<UserGroup> userGroups);
    
    default Set<User> findUsersBelongsToUserGroup(Collection<UserGroup> userGroups) {
        return userGroups.isEmpty() ? Collections.emptySet() : privateFindUsersBelongsToUserGroup(userGroups);
    }
    
}

I don't want check my collection for emptiness in service layer logic, but I don't want to expose privateFindUsersBelongsToUserGroup method. What is best practices for this case?
